items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: how astronomers detect exoplanets using the transit method
items.transcript: For most of human history, we had no way of knowing whether other stars had planets at all. Today we've confirmed thousands of them, and the workhorse technique behind that flood of discoveries is something called the transit method. The idea is surprisingly simple. When a planet passes directly between its star and us, it blocks a tiny fraction of the star's light. So from our point of view, the star dims very slightly, and then brightens again once the planet moves on. Astronomers point a telescope at a star and measure its brightness very precisely over time. If they see the light dip by the same small amount, at regular intervals, that repeating pattern is the signature of a planet orbiting the star. Now, how much the star dims tells us something useful. A bigger planet blocks more light, so a deeper dip means a larger planet. And the time between one dip and the next tells us how long the planet takes to orbit, which is essentially its year. The Kepler space telescope, launched in two thousand nine, used exactly this approach. It stared at a single patch of sky containing roughly a hundred and fifty thousand stars, watching for those faint, repeating dips. From that one patch, it found thousands of candidate planets. There is a catch, though. The method only works when a planet's orbit happens to be lined up edge-on, so that it actually crosses in front of the star from our viewpoint. Most planetary systems are tilted the wrong way, and we simply miss them. So the planets we detect are only a small fraction of those that are really out there.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A method that detects planets by measuring small dips in starlight
options.B: The design and launch history of the Kepler space telescope
options.C: How astronomers calculate the temperature of distant stars
options.D: Why most planetary systems are tilted away from Earth

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整堂课讲的是"transit method"——通过测量行星经过恒星时星光的微小下降来探测系外行星，这是贯穿全课的主旨。B错误（too_narrow），开普勒望远镜只是该方法的一个应用例子，不是课程主题。C错误（wrong_topic），课程从未讨论如何计算恒星温度。D错误（surface_word_trap），"tilted"这个细节出现在结尾，但只是说明方法的局限，并非课程主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the depth of the dip in starlight indicate?

options.A: How far the planet is from its parent star
options.B: How large the planet passing in front of the star is
options.C: How hot the surface of the planet happens to be
options.D: How many planets are orbiting the same star

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说"A bigger planet blocks more light, so a deeper dip means a larger planet"，即下降幅度反映行星的大小。A错误（topic_adjacent），课程用的是两次下降之间的时间来推断轨道，而非用下降深度推断距离。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未提到用星光下降来判断行星温度。D错误（wrong_detail），下降深度反映单颗行星的大小，并非行星数量，篡改了关键事实。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Kepler space telescope do?

options.A: It traveled close to nearby planets to photograph them directly
options.B: It measured the gravitational pull of stars on their planets
options.C: It watched one patch of sky with many stars for repeating dips
options.D: It scanned the entire sky once every year for new planets

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说开普勒"stared at a single patch of sky containing roughly a hundred and fifty thousand stars, watching for those faint, repeating dips"。A错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说望远镜飞近行星直接拍照。B错误（wrong_detail），课程讲的是测量星光亮度，而非测量引力。D错误（reversed_logic），开普勒盯着单一天区，并非每年扫描整个天空，与课程描述相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain that a planet's orbit must be lined up edge-on?

options.A: To prove that the Kepler telescope made an error in its measurements
options.B: To suggest that edge-on orbits are more common than tilted ones
options.C: To restate that planets pass directly between the star and us
options.D: To explain why the method misses most of the planets that exist

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授提出边缘对齐的要求，是为了说明这种方法的局限——大多数行星系倾斜方向不对，因此被漏掉，我们探测到的只是一小部分。A错误（wrong_purpose），教授并非在指出开普勒测量有误。B错误（over_inference），课程恰恰说大多数系统倾斜方向不对，并未说边缘对齐更常见。C错误（literal_restatement），这只是复述"行星从恒星前经过"的字面内容，没有解释教授提到这一点的目的。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how green roofs reduce urban stormwater runoff
items.transcript: Think about what happens during a heavy rainstorm in a city. The rain hits rooftops, sidewalks, and streets, and because those surfaces are hard and sealed, the water can't soak in. Instead it rushes off into the drains all at once, and sometimes the drains can't keep up, and you get flooding. One increasingly popular solution to this problem is the green roof. A green roof is exactly what it sounds like: a rooftop that's been covered with a layer of soil and living plants instead of bare tiles or tar. Now, the key thing a green roof does is slow down the water. When rain falls on a green roof, the soil and plants act like a sponge. They soak up a good portion of the rainfall and hold onto it. That water doesn't go straight into the drainage system. Instead, much of it is gradually released back into the air through the plants, or it evaporates over the following hours and days. Studies in several cities have found that a green roof can capture more than half of the rain that falls on it during a typical storm. And just as important, the water that does run off comes off more slowly and more steadily, rather than in one sudden surge. That gives the city's drains time to handle it. So green roofs don't just look pleasant or provide a little extra insulation. They quietly take pressure off the entire stormwater system, one building at a time.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How city drainage systems are designed and maintained
options.B: How green roofs help reduce stormwater runoff in cities
options.C: Why heavy rainstorms have become more frequent in cities
options.D: How plants release water vapor back into the atmosphere

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。整堂课讲绿色屋顶如何吸收并减缓雨水径流，从而减轻城市排水压力，这是主旨。A错误（wrong_topic），课程提到排水系统，但重点是绿色屋顶的作用，而非排水系统的设计维护。C错误（wrong_topic），课程从未讨论暴雨为何变得更频繁。D错误（too_narrow），植物蒸腾释水只是绿色屋顶起作用的一个细节机制，不是课程主题。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare the soil and plants of a green roof to a sponge?

options.A: To explain why green roofs need frequent watering by hand
options.B: To argue that sponges are an ideal building material for roofs
options.C: To illustrate how a green roof soaks up and holds rainwater
options.D: To describe the soft texture of the soil used on green roofs

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授用海绵的比喻是为了形象说明绿色屋顶的土壤和植物如何吸收并留住雨水，使其不立即进入排水系统。A错误（wrong_purpose），教授并非在讲屋顶需要人工浇水。B错误（over_inference），课程从未主张用海绵作建材。D错误（literal_restatement），把比喻误读为描述土壤质地，是对字面的复述，没有抓住比喻的真正用意。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did studies in several cities find about green roofs?

options.A: They eliminate the need for any drainage system at all
options.B: They release captured water instantly during a storm
options.C: They cost less to build than ordinary tar rooftops
options.D: They can capture more than half of the rain in a typical storm

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说研究发现"a green roof can capture more than half of the rain that falls on it during a typical storm"。A错误（over_generalization），课程说绿色屋顶减轻排水系统压力，但绝未说能完全取代排水系统。B错误（reversed_logic），课程强调水是被缓慢释放的，并非暴雨期间立即释放，方向相反。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未比较绿色屋顶与普通屋顶的造价。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the benefit of green roofs for a city?

options.A: Widespread use of green roofs could lower the risk of urban flooding
options.B: Green roofs are useful only on very tall office buildings
options.C: Green roofs make ordinary drains completely unnecessary
options.D: Green roofs are valued mainly for their pleasant appearance

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授说绿色屋顶"take pressure off the entire stormwater system, one building at a time"，并指出排水系统超负荷会导致洪水，暗示推广绿色屋顶能降低城市内涝风险。B错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说只对高楼有用。C错误（over_generalization），课程说减轻排水压力，并未说让排水管完全多余。D错误（reversed_logic），教授明确说绿色屋顶"don't just look pleasant"，其价值不只在外观，与该选项相反。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: the role of wetlands in flood prevention
items.transcript: We often think of wetlands as wastelands, soggy ground that's good for nothing until it's drained and built upon. But ecologically, wetlands are among the most valuable landscapes we have, and one of their quietest, most important services is flood control. Here's the basic mechanism. A wetland, whether it's a marsh, a swamp, or a floodplain, sits in a low-lying area and acts as a natural reservoir. When a river swells after heavy rain or snowmelt, the excess water spreads out into the surrounding wetland rather than racing downstream. The wetland temporarily stores that water, holding it like a giant, shallow basin. Then it releases the water slowly, over days or weeks, as the flood subsides. So the wetland flattens out the flood. It turns a sharp, dangerous peak of water into a lower, gentler, more drawn-out flow. The vegetation matters here too. Reeds, grasses, and other wetland plants create friction that slows the moving water and lets sediment settle out. Now consider what happens when we drain a wetland and replace it with farmland or pavement. The water that the wetland used to absorb now has nowhere to go. It moves downstream faster and arrives all at once, and communities further down the river face higher, more sudden floods. In fact, after some major river floods, engineers have found that restoring upstream wetlands protects downstream towns more cheaply and reliably than building taller concrete levees. So a landscape we once dismissed as useless turns out to be a piece of infrastructure, one that nature maintains for free.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why wetlands are home to such a wide variety of plant species
options.B: How engineers design concrete levees to hold back rivers
options.C: How wetlands help control flooding by storing and slowing water
options.D: Why farmers prefer to drain wetlands and build on the land

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。整堂课讲湿地如何通过储存并缓慢释放洪水来起到防洪作用，这是主旨。A错误（wrong_topic），课程未讨论湿地的物种多样性。B错误（surface_word_trap），"levees"在结尾出现，但只作对比，课程主题不是混凝土堤坝的设计。D错误（too_narrow），排干湿地建农田只是用来对比的反例，不是课程主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do wetland plants such as reeds and grasses help during a flood?

options.A: They absorb most of the flood water directly through their leaves
options.B: They block the river entirely so that water stops flowing
options.C: They pump excess water back upstream toward its source
options.D: They create friction that slows the water and lets sediment settle

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说芦苇、草等植物"create friction that slows the moving water and lets sediment settle out"。A错误（wrong_detail），课程说储水的是湿地这个浅盆，植物的作用是制造摩擦减速，并非靠叶子直接吸水。B错误（over_generalization），植物是减缓水流，并非完全挡住河流让水停止。C错误（reversed_logic），植物让水流变慢、泥沙沉降，并非把水泵回上游，方向相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to flood water after a wetland is drained and paved over?

options.A: It moves downstream faster and arrives all at once
options.B: It soaks slowly into the new farmland and pavement
options.C: It evaporates before it can reach downstream towns
options.D: It spreads out evenly across the surrounding region

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授说湿地被排干后，水"moves downstream faster and arrives all at once"，下游社区面临更高更突然的洪水。B错误（reversed_logic），铺装地面是封闭硬质表面，水无法渗入，与该选项相反。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说水在到达下游前蒸发掉。D错误（wrong_detail），课程说水集中突然到达，而非均匀铺开，篡改了关键事实。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about restoring wetlands as a flood-control strategy?

options.A: It is effective only in regions that rarely experience heavy rain
options.B: It can be a more cost-effective option than building taller levees
options.C: It works only when combined with much higher concrete levees
options.D: It has been proven to completely eliminate all downstream flooding

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说工程师发现"restoring upstream wetlands protects downstream towns more cheaply and reliably than building taller concrete levees"，暗示恢复湿地可能比建更高堤坝更划算。A错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未把效果限定在少雨地区。C错误（reversed_logic），课程把恢复湿地与建更高堤坝作对比，并未说必须与更高堤坝结合，方向相反。D错误（over_generalization），课程说湿地能减轻、削平洪峰，但绝未说能彻底消除一切下游洪水。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how geologists use ice cores to reconstruct ancient climates
items.transcript: If you want to know what Earth's climate was like thousands of years ago, long before anyone kept records, you might expect to dig through rock. But some of the best climate archives we have aren't rock at all. They're ice. In places like Antarctica and Greenland, snow falls year after year and never fully melts. Each new layer presses down on the ones below, gradually compacting them into solid ice. Over time, this builds up into an ice sheet kilometers thick, with the oldest ice at the very bottom. Geologists drill straight down and pull out long cylinders of this ice, called ice cores. And the beautiful thing is that the ice is layered, a bit like the rings of a tree. You can often see and count individual annual layers, which lets researchers assign an age to each depth. Now, why is this so valuable? As that snow fell and compacted, it trapped tiny bubbles of air. Each bubble is a sealed sample of the actual atmosphere from the year it was buried. So by carefully extracting and analyzing those bubbles, scientists can measure exactly how much carbon dioxide the air contained tens of thousands of years ago. The ice itself carries information too. The ratio of different oxygen isotopes in the water molecules depends on the temperature when the snow formed, so it acts as a thermometer for the distant past. Putting these together, ice cores have given us a continuous record of temperature and greenhouse gases stretching back hundreds of thousands of years, and that record is a cornerstone of how we understand climate change today.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why snow in Antarctica and Greenland never completely melts
options.B: How tree rings are used to measure the age of forests
options.C: How drilling equipment is built to reach deep ice sheets
options.D: How ice cores let scientists reconstruct ancient climates

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。整堂课讲地质学家如何利用冰芯重建远古气候，包括气泡里的二氧化碳和氧同位素温度记录，这是主旨。A错误（too_narrow），雪不融化只是冰层形成的背景细节，不是课程主题。B错误（surface_word_trap），树木年轮只是用来类比冰层，课程并非讲树木年轮测林龄。C错误（wrong_topic），课程提到钻取冰芯，但从未讲钻探设备如何制造。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare the layers in an ice core to the rings of a tree?

options.A: To explain that the annual layers can be counted to assign ages
options.B: To suggest that ice cores are usually taken from inside trees
options.C: To argue that trees record climate more accurately than ice does
options.D: To describe the color of the different layers within the ice

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授用树木年轮作比，是为了说明冰芯有一层层的年层，可以像数年轮一样数出来，从而给每个深度定年。B错误（wrong_purpose），这是对比喻的字面误读，冰芯并非取自树木内部。C错误（over_inference），课程从未比较树木与冰谁记录气候更准。D错误（literal_restatement），把比喻当作描述冰层颜色，是对字面的复述，没有抓住比喻的真正用意。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what can scientists learn from the air bubbles trapped in an ice core?

options.A: The exact wind speed at the time the snow fell
options.B: How much carbon dioxide the air contained long ago
options.C: The total thickness the ice sheet will eventually reach
options.D: How quickly the buried snow turned into solid ice

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说通过分析气泡，科学家能测出"how much carbon dioxide the air contained tens of thousands of years ago"。A错误（topic_adjacent），课程从未说气泡能告诉我们当时的风速。C错误（topic_adjacent），课程未提冰盖最终会有多厚。D错误（wrong_detail），课程说气泡保存的是当时大气样本，用于测二氧化碳，并非用来判断雪变成冰的速度。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the value of ice cores for understanding climate change?

options.A: They have made all other climate records obsolete and unnecessary
options.B: They are useful only for studying the climate of the last few centuries
options.C: They provide essential long-term evidence for studying climate change
options.D: They reveal future temperatures more reliably than they reveal past ones

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说冰芯提供了"a continuous record... stretching back hundreds of thousands of years"，并称其为"a cornerstone of how we understand climate change today"，暗示它是研究气候变化的重要长期证据。A错误（over_generalization），课程说冰芯是基石之一，但绝未说让其他一切记录变得多余。B错误（reversed_logic），冰芯恰恰能追溯几十万年，并非只适用于近几个世纪，方向相反。D错误（topic_adjacent），冰芯记录的是过去的温度，课程从未说它更可靠地揭示未来温度。
